---
title: Watermarks — Never Reprocess a File You've Already Seen
---

Incremental ingestion sounds simple until you hit operational reality:

- processes crash mid-run,
- files are retried by an upstream system,
- jobs restart on new machines with no local state,
- and you must avoid duplicate writes.

The core question every incremental pipeline has to answer is: *which inputs have already been
committed to the target table?*
Get that wrong and you either miss data or write it twice.
This post explains exactly how this library answers that question and why each design decision
was made the way it was.

## What a watermark is

A watermark is a persistent record of which inputs a pipeline has already processed successfully.
"Successfully" is the key word: the watermark is only updated *after* the target write completes.
That ordering is what gives you safe restarts.

Without a watermark, a pipeline restarted after a crash has no way to know whether the previous
run wrote anything to the target.
It has to choose between re-reading everything from the beginning (safe but wasteful) or
guessing at what was already done (fast but likely wrong).
A watermark removes the ambiguity.

## How this library stores watermark state

The watermark is a Delta table with one row per processed file.
By default it lives under `<target>/.watermark`, co-located with the data it tracks.
Each row records the ingested file path and the UTC timestamp when it was written:

```
file_path                              | ingested_at
---------------------------------------|----------------------------
/data/raw/customers/2024-01-01.parquet | 2024-01-01 08:00:01+00:00
/data/raw/customers/2024-01-02.parquet | 2024-01-02 08:00:03+00:00
```

At the start of each run, the library loads all `file_path` values into a set.
Any file already in that set is skipped.
Only the remainder — the *new* files — are scanned, transformed, and written to the target.
Once the target write completes without error, the new paths are appended to the watermark.

Storing this in Delta rather than a plain file or a database has practical advantages.
Delta writes are atomic: either the append commits fully or it does not appear at all.
The table is also queryable with `pl.scan_delta`, so you can inspect or audit it directly
without any special tooling.

## The write-before-watermark ordering

The most important property of the implementation is the sequence of operations:

1. Scan new files.
2. Apply the transform.
3. Write to the target table.
4. Write to the watermark table.

Steps 3 and 4 are deliberately separate.
If the process crashes between them, the watermark still shows the files as unprocessed.
The next run will pick them up again and write them a second time.
For append tables, this produces a duplicate row.
For upsert tables (where `merge_on` is set), re-processing the same file just produces
identical merge matches and the table ends up in the same state as if the file had been
processed only once.

The technical name for this property is **at-least-once** delivery: every file is guaranteed
to be processed, possibly more than once.
The opposite — **exactly-once** — requires a distributed transaction across both writes,
which Delta Lake does not provide across two separate tables.
At-least-once combined with idempotent writes (i.e. upsert) is the standard practical
substitute.

## Day-to-day controls

`IncrementalPipeline` exposes three operational helpers that are useful in production:

**`run(dry_run=True)`** lists the files that *would* be processed without reading or writing
anything.
This is useful before a first run on a large backlog, or after a schema change, to verify
that the pipeline will pick up exactly the files you expect.

**`status()`** returns the watermark table as an eager `DataFrame`.
You can filter and inspect it directly to answer questions like "when was this file last
ingested?" or "how many files were processed in the last 24 hours?".

**`reset()`** deletes all rows from the watermark table, making the next `run()` treat every
file as new.
This is useful when you want to re-derive the target table from scratch — for example after
changing the transform logic or recovering from a bad write.

## Delta source variant: version watermarks

When `file_format="delta"`, the source is another Delta table and the pipeline reads it via
**Change Data Feed** (CDF) — a feature that exposes only the rows that changed since a given
version.

In this mode, tracking file paths does not make sense because there are no individual files
to track.
Instead, the watermark records the latest source **version** that was successfully processed:

```
version | committed_at
--------|----------------------------
0       | 2024-01-01 08:00:01+00:00
3       | 2024-01-02 08:00:03+00:00
```

On each run, the library reads the max version from the watermark, fetches all CDF changes
from `max_version + 1` up to the current source version, processes them, and appends the new
version number to the watermark.
If there are no new versions, the run returns immediately without touching the target.

The concept is exactly the same as file-path watermarking.
Only the unit of progress changes: file paths become version numbers.

## Why this matters

Without watermarks, incremental ingestion has no memory.
Every run that wants to avoid full rewrites has to implement its own notion of "what did I
already do?" — usually through timestamp filters, filename patterns, or ad hoc deduplication
downstream.
Those approaches are fragile, hard to audit, and tend to break quietly.

A persistent, auditable watermark table makes the pipeline's progress an observable fact.
You can inspect it, reset it, and reason about it independently of the target table.
That is what makes retries safe and idempotency a property of the pipeline contract rather
than something each transform function has to enforce for itself.
